factions.name: Social Liberal Wing
factions.description: Progressive on social issues, interventionist on economy, strong public programs

keyPositions.healthcare: Universal public system, pharmacare, dental care

factions.historicalContext: Dominant under Pierre Trudeau (1970s-80s), resurgent under Trudeau Jr.

factions.name: Centrist Pragmatists
factions.description: Balance efficiency with social programs, moderate on most issues, incremental change

keyPositions.healthcare: Public system with private options, modest funding increases
keyPositions.labor: Work with unions, not against them

factions.historicalContext: Dominated Canadian politics 1993-2006, currently strong faction

factions.name: Bay Street Liberals

keyPositions.healthcare: More private sector involvement, user fees

factions.historicalContext: Influential on economic policy, weaker on social issues

factions.name: Civil Libertarian Liberals
factions.description: Strong on civil rights and privacy, skeptical of government power, progressive on freedoms

keyPositions.lgbtq: Full rights expansion, no religious exemptions

factions.historicalContext: Smaller faction, influential on justice/privacy issues

factions.name: Socialist Left
factions.description: Left-wing, anti-capitalist, supports worker control, radical social change

keyPositions.labor: Strong union support, worker control of production
keyPositions.environment: Radical climate action, ban fossil fuels

factions.historicalContext: Present since CCF founding, minority faction within NDP

factions.name: Social Democrats
factions.description: Left-leaning but accepts capitalism, strong public programs, worker power
factions.historicalContext: Dominant faction, provides NDP mainstream appeal

factions.name: Progressive Identity Politics
factions.description: Focus on social justice, identity issues, intersectionality, activism

factions.name: Economic Nationalist NDP
factions.description: Left-wing economically, nationalist on trade/sovereignty, Canadian focus

keyPositions.trade: Skeptical of free trade, protect Canadian industries
keyPositions.resources: Resource nationalism, keep wealth in Canada

factions.historicalContext: Present in Western Canada, less prominent nationally

factions.name: Social Conservative Wing
factions.description: Right-wing economically and socially, traditional values, law and order

keyPositions.lgbtq: Oppose expansion of rights, religious exemptions
keyPositions.law-order: Law and order priority, tough on crime

factions.historicalContext: Influential since Reform/Alliance merger, strong Western support

factions.name: Red Tory / Progressive Conservative
factions.description: Market-oriented but pragmatic, some social spending, moderate on social issues

keyPositions.healthcare: Public system but more efficiency focus
keyPositions.social: Pragmatic on social issues, not ideological

factions.historicalContext: Declining faction, stronger under Progressive Conservatives

factions.name: Libertarian Right
factions.description: Strong free market, minimal government, individual liberty, low taxes
factions.historicalContext: Present since Alliance days, strong in Western Canada

factions.name: Western Populist / Sovereigntist
factions.description: Right-wing economically and socially, strong Canadian/Western nationalism, anti-establishment

keyPositions.sovereignty: Strong Canadian sovereignty, skeptical of globalism

factions.historicalContext: Strong in Western Canada, growing appeal across party

contradictions.name: Housing Affordability vs. Anti-Development
contradictions.description: You support affordable housing but oppose market-led housing supply increases.
contradictions.explanation: Supporting affordable housing while opposing density and development typically leads to higher costs — you may want housing to be cheaper but through public provision rather than market mechanisms.
contradictions.condition: answers[3] !== undefined && answers[3] <= 1 && answers[30] !== undefined && answers[30] <= 1

resolutions.name: Public Housing Route
resolutions.text: Commit to public and non-profit housing construction as the primary supply mechanism.

resolutions.name: Market-Left Hybrid
resolutions.text: Accept some private development alongside strong rent controls and social housing.

contradictions.name: Climate Action vs. Oil Expansion
contradictions.description: You support climate action but also support expanded oil sands or Trans Mountain.
contradictions.explanation: Favouring both aggressive emissions reduction and expanded fossil fuel infrastructure creates a fundamental tension — the IEA says no new oil fields are consistent with 1.5°C.
contradictions.condition: answers[7] !== undefined && answers[7] <= 1 && answers[56] !== undefined && answers[56] >= 2

resolutions.name: Just Transition
resolutions.text: Accept that managed decline of oil sands is necessary for climate targets.

resolutions.name: Technology Optimist
resolutions.text: Believe carbon capture and clean tech will allow both oil extraction and emissions reduction.

contradictions.name: High Immigration + Housing Crisis
contradictions.description: You support high immigration levels but also want affordable housing.
contradictions.explanation: Record immigration (1.2M+/yr) puts significant pressure on housing demand. These goals create tension without a major supply-side response.
contradictions.condition: answers[30] !== undefined && answers[30] <= 1 && answers[3] !== undefined && answers[3] <= 1

resolutions.name: Supply-Side Investment
resolutions.text: Massive public housing construction can accommodate both goals simultaneously.

resolutions.name: Managed Intake
resolutions.text: Moderate immigration levels temporarily until housing supply catches up.

contradictions.name: Individual Liberty vs. Drug Enforcement
contradictions.description: You favour civil liberties and limited state power, but strict drug enforcement.
contradictions.explanation: The war on drugs involves extensive state coercion, surveillance, and incarceration — typically opposed by civil libertarians across the political spectrum.
contradictions.condition: answers[24] !== undefined && answers[24] <= 1 && answers[13] !== undefined && answers[13] >= 2

resolutions.name: Harm Reduction Liberty
resolutions.text: Apply libertarian principles consistently — decriminalize personal drug use.

resolutions.name: Community Standards
resolutions.text: Distinguish between personal freedoms and community protection standards.

contradictions.name: Fiscal Conservatism vs. Strong Military
contradictions.description: You want significant deficit reduction but also want to increase defence spending.
contradictions.explanation: Meeting NATO's 2% GDP target would add ~$15B annually to federal spending. This is hard to reconcile with urgent deficit reduction.
contradictions.condition: answers[4] !== undefined && answers[4] >= 3 && answers[35] !== undefined && answers[35] >= 2

resolutions.name: Reallocation
resolutions.text: Cut other spending to fund defence within a balanced budget.

resolutions.name: Phased Approach
resolutions.text: Accept that defence investment requires a longer fiscal timeline.

contradictions.name: Free Trade vs. Supply Management
contradictions.description: You support open free trade but also want to keep supply management.
contradictions.explanation: Supply management is a quota and tariff system that contradicts free trade principles. Every trade partner demands its removal. You can't fully hold both positions.
contradictions.condition: answers[31] !== undefined && answers[31] <= 1 && answers[44] !== undefined && answers[44] <= 1

resolutions.name: Managed Trade
resolutions.text: Accept that strategic sectors can be exempt from free trade rules.

resolutions.name: Compensated Reform
resolutions.text: Phase out supply management with transition support for farmers.

contradictions.name: Skeptical of State Power vs. Carbon Tax Support
contradictions.description: You distrust government authority but support a carbon pricing mechanism.
contradictions.explanation: Carbon pricing is a form of state-imposed economic intervention. Libertarian-minded conservatives sometimes hold this position but it creates philosophical tension.
contradictions.condition: answers[24] !== undefined && answers[24] >= 2 && answers[7] !== undefined && answers[7] <= 1

resolutions.name: Market Mechanism
resolutions.text: A revenue-neutral carbon tax is actually more market-friendly than direct regulation.

resolutions.name: Voluntary Alternative
resolutions.text: Prefer voluntary carbon offsets and technology investment over mandatory pricing.

contradictions.name: Strong Nationalism vs. Free Trade
contradictions.description: You prioritize Canadian sovereignty and nationalism but also support open free trade.
contradictions.explanation: Free trade agreements require ceding sovereignty over economic policy. Trump's tariffs show that trade dependence is a sovereignty risk — nationalists typically prefer protectionism.
contradictions.condition: answers[31] !== undefined && answers[31] <= 1 && answers[32] !== undefined && answers[32] >= 2

resolutions.name: Economic Nationalist
resolutions.text: Consistent nationalism requires protecting domestic industries through managed trade.

resolutions.name: Sovereign Market
resolutions.text: Free trade is compatible with sovereignty when you have economic leverage.
